coredata.dc:title: Synchronously integration of Co, Fe dual-metal doping in Ru@C and CDs for boosted water splitting performances in alkaline media
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Mingxi
Feng, Tanglue
Chen, Yixin
Liu, Junjun
Zhao, Xiaohuan
Yang, Bai
coredata.dc:description: The unsatisfactory performances and complicated synthesis procedures greatly impeded the large-scale industrial applications of various electrocatalysts. Herein, two different functional electrocatalysts were simultaneously synthesized within one-pot reactor through hydrothermal method. The Co, Fe co-doped carbon dots (CoFe-CDs) with electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activities and carbon-supported Co, Fe co-doped Ru networks (CoFeRu@C) with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activities, were directly obtained from the supernatant and the precipitation, respectively. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of dual-metal doping, the CoFeRu@C exhibited an extremely low overpotential of 4 mV for HER in 1 M KOH electrolyte, which was the lowest value among the previously reported electrocatalysts, and the CoFe-CDs showed excellent OER performance with an overpotential of 320 mV. Moreover, only 1.59 V was needed to achieve 10 mA cm−2 for overall water-splitting. This work provided an innovative approach for simply creating diverse electrocatalysts and effectively regulating the catalytic activities by introducing multi-metal dopants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-loaded ruthenium
Carbon dots
Bimetallic doping
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of CoFe-CDs/CoFeRu@C catalysts and electrocatalytic OER/HER applications.

a) SEM image of CoFeRu@C (inset shows high-magnification image). b) TEM image of CoFeRu@C (inset shows high-magnification image). c) HRTEM image of CoFeRu@C focused on the edge. d) XRD pattern of CoFeRu@C. e) XPS analysis of CoFeRu@C in full spectra and f) the high-resolution Ru 3p peaks. g) EDS elemental mapping images of CoFeRu@C.

a) TEM image and size distribution histogram of CoFe-CDs (inset shows HRTEM image of CoFe-CDs). b) FT-IR spectra of CoFe-CDs. c) UV–vis absorption, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of CoFe-CDs (insets show photographs of CoFe-CDs solution under daylight and UV irradiation). d) XRD pattern of CoFe-CDs. e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p and f) Fe 2p of CoFe-CDs.

Electrocatalytic HER performance tests in 1 M KOH media. a) Comparison LSV curves of CoFe-CDs, CoFe@C, Ru@C, 20 % Pt/C, FeRu@C, CoRu@C and CoFeRu@C. b) Tafel slopes, c) Cdl values obtained from capacitive current density against scan rates, and d) The calculated relative energy diagram of the HER for the Ru-G and CoFeRu-G models. e) LSV curves of CoFeRu@C before and after 1000th cycles sweep. f) i-t curve of CoFeRu@C at an overpotential of -4 mV.

Electrocatalytic OER performance tests in 1 M KOH media. a) Comparison LSV curves of CoFeRu@C, G-CDs, Fe-CDs, Co-CDs, RuO2 and CoFe-CDs. b) Tafel slopes, c) Cdl values obtained from capacitive current density against scan rates, and d) EIS analysis of G-CDs, Fe-CDs, Co-CDs and CoFe-CDs. e) LSV curves of CoFe-CDs before and after 1000th cycles sweep. f) i-t curve of CoFe-CDs at an overpotential of 320 mV.
a) LSV curve of the CoFe-CDs/CoFeRu@C catalysts in 1 M KOH for overall water-splitting with a two-electrode system. b) i-t curve of the CoFe-CDs/CoFeRu@C catalysts at an applied potential of 1.59 V.

head.ce:title: Synchronously integration of Co, Fe dual-metal doping in Ru@C and CDs for boosted water splitting performances in alkaline media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, P. R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unsatisfactory performances and complicated synthesis procedures greatly impeded the large-scale industrial applications of various electrocatalysts. Herein, two different functional electrocatalysts were simultaneously synthesized within one-pot reactor through hydrothermal method. The Co, Fe co-doped carbon dots (CoFe-CDs) with electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) activities and carbon-supported Co, Fe co-doped Ru networks (CoFeRu@C) with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activities, were directly obtained from the supernatant and the precipitation, respectively. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of dual-metal doping, the CoFeRu@C exhibited an extremely low overpotential of 4 mV for HER in 1 M KOH electrolyte, which was the lowest value among the previously reported electrocatalysts, and the CoFe-CDs showed excellent OER performance with an overpotential of 320 mV. Moreover, only 1.59 V was needed to achieve 10 mA cm−2 for overall water-splitting. This work provided an innovative approach for simply creating diverse electrocatalysts and effectively regulating the catalytic activities by introducing multi-metal dopants.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid growth in energy consumptions, impending depletion of fossil fuels and global CO2 emissions have become the most severe crisis of human survival and continuation. Hydrogen (H2), as a renewable, zero-emissive and earth-abundant energy resource, is believed capable of solving the energy and environmental problems [1–4]. Among the many industrial routes towards hydrogen energy, the electrochemical generation of high-purity H2 via hydrogen evolution reaction (HER, E° = 0 V vs RHE) has been considered as the most promising alternative technology [5–10]. However, the practical application of electrocatalytic overall water-splitting for massive hydrogen production is limited due to the large overpotential of oxygen evolution reaction (OER, E° = 1.23 V vs RHE), which is a kinetically sluggish half-reaction with multi-step four-electron transfer process [11–15].

Nowadays, Pt-group metals and Ir-/Ru-based oxides are still regarded as the best HER and OER electrocatalysts, respectively, while their scarcity and high-cost have restricted the large-scale sustainable applications in the future [16–19]. In recent years, plenty of transition metals (Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Mo, Ru, etc.)-based electrocatalysts have been developed [20–27], but the poor activity and unsatisfactory stability made them unable to completely replace commercial noble metal catalysts, so that doping or alloying nonprecious transition metals within noble metals became an effective strategy to regulate and modify the electronic structures for achieving optimal performance, meanwhile reducing the usage amount of precious metal [28–37]. Moreover, combination with carbon material (i.e., graphene [38,39], carbon nanotube [40,41], carbon nanosheets [42–44], carbon dots [45–51]) was also be proved as an effective way to improve the electrocatalytic activity and durability, due to enhanced conductivity and charge transfer in metal/carbon composites. The HER performance had been greatly optimized by researchers with overpotential as low as 10 mV, while there still existed some issues for nearly all the noble and non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts. Typically, the complicated synthesis procedure and high-temperature calcination (usually 400−1000 °C) are generally required for high crystallinity and favorable interface interaction of the hybrid catalysts, which is contradictory to energy saving and emission reduction [18,19,24,25,28–30,38–46,49]. It is of great significance to design and explore high-efficiency HER and OER electrocatalysts through simple and green synthetic routes. Especially, it will be highly meaningful if diverse electrocatalysts with different catalytic functionality can be obtained through single-step and one-pot reaction.

Herein, for the first time, carbon-loaded Co, Fe co-doped ruthenium nanoparticles networks (CoFeRu@C) with superior electrocatalytic HER activities and Co, Fe co-doped carbon dots (CoFe-CDs) with OER activities are simultaneously synthesized within one-pot hydrothermal reactor under mild condition. The active metal ions doping strategy is synchronously adopted here to efficiently facilitate the electrocatalytic activities of Ru@C and CDs. Benefitting from the synergistic effect of bimetallic dopants and the large specific surface areas, the HER overpotential of CoFeRu@C at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 is as low as 4 mV in 1 M KOH, which to the best of our knowledge, is the lowest value among the previously reported noble and non-noble metal-based HER electrocatalysts. Also, a low overpotential of 320 mV (10 mA cm−2) for OER is obtained by CoFe-CDs in 1 M KOH solution, which is comparable to many reported complex metal/carbon hybrid electrocatalysts. The electrocatalytic performance and durability of CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs were both superior to the commercial Pt/C and RuO2, respectively. Furthermore, electrochemical overall water splitting is achieved with only 1.59 V at 10 mA cm−2 based on the CoFeRu@C/CoFe-CDs system, indicating the great potential of CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs serving as simply-prepared, low-cost and high-efficiency novel electrocatalysts. It is believed that the strategy of dual-transition metal ions doping may provide an effective and direct approach for improving the performances of various electro- or photo-catalysts in the future.

Cobalt(II) gluconate hydrate (C12H22CoO14·xH2O) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Ferrous gluconate dihydrate (C12H22FeO14·2H2O), D-(+)-gluconic acid δ-lactone (C6H10O6) and ruthenium(III) chloride trihydrate (RuCl3·3H2O) were purchased from Macklin. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent. Nafion (5 %) ethanol solution was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without any further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the whole experiments.

The CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs were simultaneously synthesized within one-pot through hydrothermal method. Briefly, cobalt/ferrous gluconate (0.25 mmol) and ruthenium(III) chloride (RuCl3, 0.05-0.5 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL water, then the solution was transferred to a poly tetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave followed by being heated in an oven at 200 °C for 4 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at a high speed of 18,000 rpm for 20 min to completely separate the CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs. The supernatant was filtered using 0.22 μm millipore filter and then dialyzed against water with a dialysis tube 500−1000 Da) for 48 h in order to obtain the purified CoFe-CDs. The precipitate was further centrifugal washing with water for 2 times to remove the impurity and CoFe-CDs, and then the CoFeRu@C was obtained. Typically, the CoFe-CDs in this work was referred to the sample with a cobalt/ferrous gluconate feeding ratio of 7:3, and the CoFeRu@C was referred to the sample with Ru feeding amount of 0.15 mmol if not specified. The final production yield was ca. 27 % and 20 % for CoFe-CDs and CoFeRu@C, respectively, the nearly 50 percent of high total output was because of the adequately use of both the solution and the precipitation of the products.
The G-CDs, Fe-CDs, Co-CDs and Ru@C, FeRu@C, CoRu@C were synthesized for comparison through the same procedure by using sole gluconic acid, ferrous gluconate or cobalt gluconate, respectively.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on Hitachi SU-8020 and JEOL JEM-2100 F, respectively. The elemental mapping images were recorded on energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) combined with TEM. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on PANalytical B.V. Empyrean small-angle diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). The total metal content was measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP) on Agilent 725. Photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on Shimadzu RF-5301 PC and 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed on Nicolet Avatar 360FTIR spectrophotometer.

For OER, 15 μL of CoFe-CDs solution (4 mg/mL) was dropped onto the glassy carbon electrode (GCE, with a diameter of 3 mm, electrode area of 0.0707 cm2). Then the solution was dried under 60 °C and 2.5 μL of Nafion ethanol solution (2 wt%) was covered on the catalyst. For HER, the CoFeRu@C powder was first dispersed in water (3 mg/mL), and the mixed solution was sonicated for 30 min. After that 20 μL of the mixture was dropped onto the GC electrode and further dried under 60 °C, then 2.5 μL of Nafion ethanol solution (2 wt%) was covered on the catalyst. The catalysts loading amount was calculated as 0.848 mg/cm2 for CoFe-CDs and CoFeRu@C. For the comparison experiments, the loading amounts of RuO2 and 20 % Pt/C was the same as CoFe-CDs and CoFeRu@C.

All the electrochemical measurements were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C, CH Instrument, Shanghai). The OER and HER measurements were carried out on a standard three-electrode electrochemical quartz cell with N2-saturated 1 M KOH solution at room temperature. A calomel electrode (Hg/Hg2Cl2 in saturated KCl solution) and a graphite rode were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve was recorded at a scan rate of 1 mV/s. The potential against the calomel electrode (SCE) was converted to the potential versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the equation E vs RHE = E vs SCE + 0.2412 V + 0.0591×pH at 25 °C), and the overpotential was calculated η = E j=10 - 1.23 V for OER and η = E j=10 - 0 V for HER, respectively. The electrochemically active surface area was estimated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) method, the voltage range was from 1.23 V to 1.33 V for OER and 0.2 V to 0.3 V for HER (E vs RHE), respectively, and the difference value of the current density (Δj) between anodic and cathodic sweep was measured at the middle potential with the scan rate increasing from 5 mV/s to 40 mV/s. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was obtained from the slope of the fitted linear curve. The Tafel slope was calculated according to the equation (η = a + b log j, η is overpotential, b is Tafel slope and j is current density). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectroscopy was performed in a frequency range from 100 mHz to 10 kHz. The turnover frequency (TOF) value was calculated from the equation TOF = (JA) / (4Fn) (OER) and TOF = (JA) / (2Fn) (HER), where j is the current density at a certain overpotential, A is the surface area of the electrode (0.0707 cm2), 4 is the mole of transferred electrons for one mole O2 generation, 2 is the mole of transferred electrons for one mole H2 generation, F is the Faraday constant 96485 C mol−1), n is the moles of active sites (TOFs were calculated based on the assumption that all metal ions in the samples are catalytically active). The durability of the electrocatalysts was evaluated by the comparison of the LSV curves before and after 1000 CV cycles at 50 mV/s, and recording the change of the current density with continuous applied voltage at the overpotential (i-t curve).

The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof exchange-correlation functional and a 400-eV cutoff for the plane-wave basis set are employed to perform all the density functional theory (DFT) computations within the frame of Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP). The projector-augmented plane wave (PAW) is used to describe the electron-ion interactions. The Gamma k-point is employed for geometric optimization. The convergence threshold is set as 10−6 eV in energy and 0.05 eV/Å in force. For all the calculations of slab models, the symmetrization is switched off.

The HER is assumed to involve two elementary reaction steps accompanied by proton expulsion, as indicated below:
(1) 2H+ + * + 2e− — H* + H+ + e−
(2) H* + H+ +e− — * + H2

The free energy of the HER is calculated according to the equation ΔG = ΔEH + ΔZPE – TΔS, where ΔEH is obtained by the computation of the hydrogen binding energy of the geometrical structures. ΔZPE and ΔS are the zero point energy changes determined by employing the computed vibrational frequencies and standard tables for the reactants and products in the gas phase.

In the present work, the two different functional materials (i.e., CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs) were ingeniously designed and simultaneously synthesized within one-pot hydrothermal reactor (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, cobalt gluconate, ferrous gluconate and ruthenium (III) chloride (RuCl3) were dissolved in aqueous solution, following by one-step hydrothermal treatment under mild reaction condition (200 °C, 4 h) and no high-temperature calcination was required, which was conducive to reduce energy consumption for industrial applications. The gluconate salt, as a kind of nutritional pharmacy agent that contain carbon, oxygen and metal ions, could be utilized as raw materials for both CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs. During the hydrothermal process, two main reactions occurred as below: 1) the gluconic acid portions serving as the carbonaceous precursor of CDs underwent dehydration and carbonization process, accompanied by in-situ doping of Co, Fe ions, which derived CoFe-CDs in the supernatant; 2) the abundant hydroxyl groups of gluconate salts adequately reduced Ru3+ ions to Ru° nanoparticles (NPs), while the cobalt and ferrous gluconate carbonized and fused into carbon networks which encapsulated the Co, Fe-doped Ru NPs, resulting in the formation of CoFeRu@C in the precipitate.

The CoFeRu@C was obtained by simply centrifugal washing the precipitate at the bottom of the hydrothermal autoclave. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and TEM were used to explore the morphology of CoFeRu@C (Fig. 1 a-b). It was found that CoFeRu@C appeared as micrometer-scale cross-linking networks consisted of nano-scale particles with rough surface rich of small protuberances (Fig. 1a-b insets), which was beneficial to improve the specific surface area for electrocatalytic reaction. HRTEM image clearly showed Ru NPs were embedded in carbon matrix (Fig. S1), the lattice fringes of 0.272 nm and 0.361 nm found in the outer layer were close to the spacing of (100) and (002) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [49]. The relatively dark Ru particle was observed with a lattice fringe of 0.205 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of hexagonal Ru (Fig. 1c) [46]. As expected, XRD pattern further proved the hybrid structure of Ru@C (Fig. 1d), the broad peak at approximately 20° was due to the graphite carbon, and the diffraction peaks at 38.3°, 44.0°, 58.4°, 69.1°, 78.1°, 85.2° could be ascribed as the (100), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112) planes of hexagonal Ru (PDF no. 06-0663), respectively, indicating the presence of carbon loaded Ru composite. The morphology evolution of CoFeRu@C during the hydrothermal process was recorded at different reaction time (Fig. S2). It could be seen that the black Ru particles gradually generated and aggregated from the internal of the carbon matrix in the first half of reaction time, and the completely formed CoFeRu@C continued to grow, reaching micro-level in the second half, eventually resulting in the formation of the catalysts. The co-existence of Ru, C and the co-doping of Co, Fe was further determined from XPS results, as shown in Fig. 1e. It could be seen that the electronic configuration signal of Ru 3p3/2 and Ru 3p1/2 aroused with peaks at the binding energy of 462.5 eV and 484.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 1f), also the peaks at 280.7 eV (Ru 3d5/2) and 284.0 (Ru 3d3/2) eV were overlapped in C 1s spectrum, confirming the existence of Ru°. For the Co, Fe dopants, the deconvoluted Co 2p spectra showed two typical peaks belonging to Co 2p3/2 at 782.1 eV and Co 2p1/2 at 798.3 eV with satellite peaks (Fig. S3a), indicating the ionic doped state of Co2+ within Ru [52]. In addition, the Fe dopant was detected as Fe3+ form because of the Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks at 712.9 eV and 726.9 eV in Fe 2p spectra, respectively, as well as the distinct satellite peaks (Fig. S3b) [53]. The stability of Co and Fe dopants that maintaining ionic states was probably originated from the strong coordination interaction and protective ability of carboxylate groups from gluconate salts and the relatively mild conditions of hydrothermal reaction [54,55]. The C 1s spectra showed the peak of sp2/sp3 carbon (CC/CC) at a binding energy of 284.6 eV, and oxygen-containing groups (CO/CO) at 285.8 eV and 288.4 eV (Fig. S3c), which also appeared in the O 1s spectra (Fig. S3d). The atomic percentage of C, O, Ru, Co and Fe was calculated as 79.2 %, 11.2 %, 4.8 %, 2.8 % and 2.0 %, respectively, according to the XPS peak area ratio. In addition, inductively coupled plasma atom emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) analysis showed that the total metal content was 1.18 wt% for Co, 1.03 wt% for Fe and 3.75 wt% for Ru, respectively. Moreover, the element mapping images illustrated the homogeneous dispersion of C, O, Co, Fe and Ru elements (Fig. 1g), also revealing the structures of carbon loaded Co, Fe-doped Ru.

The CoFe-CDs was produced from the solution after removal of the precipitation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image revealed that the as-prepared CoFe-CDs had well-dispersed spherical structures with an average size of 3.0 nm (Fig. 2 a), and distinct lattice fringes of 0.21 nm were clearly shown in high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2a inset), which could be corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [56]. The PL and UV–vis spectra revealed the classic optical properties of CoFe-CDs similar to other reported works, that was, the emission of blue fluorescence under UV light excitation (Fig. 2b). The Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum showed the stretching vibration of OH at 3394 cm−1, CO at 1622 cm−1 and COC at 1056 cm−1 [57,58] (Fig. 2c), confirming the occurrence of dehydration reactions between hydroxyl groups and the formation of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were utilized to further investigate the chemical composition and the metal dopants of CoFe-CDs. XPS analysis verified the existence of C, O, Co and Fe in CoFe-CDs (Fig. S4a), the extremely weak Ru signal was because that the vast majority of Ru3+ was reduced into the precipitated CoFeRu@C and the rest would be removed by dialysis treatment (Fig. S4b), while the Co and Fe dopants could be reserved on the surfaces of CoFe-CDs against dialysis due to the complexation and protection of carboxylate functional groups. The high-resolution Co 2p and Fe 2p spectra were shown in Fig. 2e and f, which also revealed the doping states were Co2+ and Fe3+ ions in CoFe-CDs based on the identical peak positions and shake-up satellite peaks. The C 1s and O 1s spectra of CoFe-CDs were given in Fig. S4 c and d, which was similar with that of CoFeRu@C probably because they were derived from the same raw materials. Accordingly, the relative atomic contents of C, O, Co and Fe were calculated as 45.7 %, 42.8 %, 7.4 % and 4.1 %, respectively. Also, the mass fraction of Co and Fe was measured from ICP analysis as 4.61 wt% and 4.34 wt%, respectively. Furthermore, there was only a broad diffraction peak at around 20° represented in XRD pattern, corresponding to graphite carbon, and no characteristic peaks of Co/Fe-based metal cluster or oxide appeared (Fig. 2d), indicating that no generation of such substances except for Co and Fe ions.

The electrocatalytic HER activity of CoFeRu@C was firstly evaluated based on a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell in 1 M KOH media, the used reference electrode was calibrated before tests (Fig. S5). Surprisingly, the overpotential of CoFeRu@C was obtained as low as 4 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which was substantially lower than that of the commercial Pt/C catalysts (21 mV), and to the best of our knowledge, was the lowest value among all the reported noble and non-noble metal-based HER electrocatalysts (Table S1). As comparison, several LSV tests of control catalysts were also carried out to clarify the mechanism of the breakthrough of HER performance in CoFeRu@C. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the CoFeRu@C showed the most superior HER activity with LSV curve of the fastest decay rate and the smallest overpotential. The CoFe-CDs and CoFe@C synthesized in the absence of Ru had extremely poor activities with overpotential of 334 mV and 232 mV at 10 mA cm−2, respectively, demonstrating the intrinsic HER activity was originated from Ru. The Ru@C possessed a relatively low overpotential of 86 mV, yet still much higher than that of CoFeRu@C, indicating the essential factor of Co, Fe dopants to arouse the catalytic activity of Ru. As the feeding amounts of Ru increased from 0.05 mmol to 0.15 mmol, the electrocatalytic HER performances of the as-prepared CoFeRu@C initially improved because of the addition of Ru sites (Fig. S7), while the activity gradually decreased as Ru contents further increased probably due to the excessive ratio of Ru/C, leading to the unoptimized hybrid structure for HER reactions. Moreover, noting that single-doping with Fe or Co could be able to remarkably reduce the overpotential to 18 mV (FeRu@C) and 14 mV (CoRu@C), respectively, and dual-doping further to 4 mV (CoFeRu@C). The Co/Fe feeding ratio had inconspicuous influence on the overpotential of CoFeRu@C, indicating the synergistic effect was regardless of the dual-metal proportion (Fig. S8). This result verified that the strategy of active metal doping could significantly improve the electrochemical activity of Ru@C via optimization of electronic structure and charge conductivity. Typically, when multiple transition metal were utilized for co-doping, the modulation and synergistic effect among metals could magnify the impact, resulting in the further enhancement of eletrocatalytic properties. According to fitted Tafel equation (η = blog j + a), the Tafel slope of CoFeRu@C was calculated as 33.7 mV/dec, which was slightly lower than 34.5 mV/dec of CoRu@C and 35.5 mV/dec of FeRu@C, and all three values were lower than that of Pt/C (49.1 mV/dec), while the Ru@C without introduction of Co and Fe had high Tafel slope of 95.7 mV/dec (Fig. 3b), also proving the speculation that single- and dual-doping of active metal could both accelerate the HER dynamics of Ru@C. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) values (Fig. 3c), which could reflect the amounts of active sites of the electrocatalysts, were determined by measuring the capacitive current density at different scan rates (Fig. S9). The specific ESCA of CoFeRu@C could be calculated according to Cdl, which was more than 10 times higher than Ru@C (Table S2), suggesting the feasibility of the Co, Fe doping strategy to raise the number of active sites for HER. Moreover, the Nyquist plots of FeRu@C, CoRu@C and CoFeRu@C had obviously smaller circle radius than that of Ru@C (Fig. S10), which meant the incorporation of Co, Fe dopants within CoFeRu@C could substantially improve the charge transfer capability. The turnover frequency (TOF), which reflected the intrinsic activities of catalysts, was calculated at the certain overpotential (Fig. S11). The CoFeRu@C showed the largest TOF value (0.50 s-1, η = 0.125 V) among the control samples (0.38 s-1 for CoRu@C, 0.31 s-1 for FeRu@C and 0.13 s-1 for Ru@C, respectively) and commercial Pt/C (0.35 s-1), further demonstrating the boosted activity of CoFeRu@C [59]. Furthermore, the origin of such excellent HER activity of CoFeRu@C was investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. A simplified structure model of a Ru cluster comprised 13 Ru atoms attached on mono-layer graphene (Ru-G) was simulated for Ru@C (Fig. S12), and a pair of charged Co, Fe was adhered upon the surface of Ru cluster, which was specified as CoFeRu-G (Fig. S13). Generally, the value of |ΔGH*| was used as one of the key catalytic descriptor in theoretical prediction of the HER activity and the optimal value should be close to 0 [25]. Based on the models, the free energy of HER (ΔGH*) was calculated as -0.45 eV for Ru-G and -0.32 eV for CoFeRu-G, respectively (Fig. 3d), which proved that the involvement of Co, Fe could help reduce the HER energy barrier. When one H2O molecule was adsorbed on the Co/Fe sites of the Ru surface, the charge-density difference maps of CoFeRu-G model also showed a more sufficient electrons transfer process from the H2O to Ru and then to the graphene plane than that of Ru-G (Fig. S14), and the electron transfer number was 0.0831 for Ru-G and 0.0972 for CoFeRu-G, respectively. The theoretical results revealed that the Co, Fe dopants within Ru@C could be conducive to both the adsorption and the dissocation of H2O molecule, which led to the unprecedented HER performance of CoFeRu@C. In addition, the CoFeRu@C exhibited decent durability in alkaline media with only 4 mV increment of overpotential after 1000 CV cycles, and the current density of CoFeRu@C also maintained quite stable with only 5.7 % loss under 12 h continuous voltage output, while the commercial Pt/C suffered a current density loss of 36.9 %. Moreover, the morphology and chemical structure of CoFeRu@C remained almost unchanged after electrocatalytic HER process (Fig. S15), indicating the great potential of CoFeRu@C serving as novel high-performance, extremely stable and simply prepared HER electrocatalysts.

Besides, the eletrocatalytic OER properties of CoFe-CDs were also explored in 1 M KOH media by three-electrode system. From the comparative linear scan curves (LSV) of differently doped CDs (Fig. 4 a), it was shown that the metal-free G-CDs from gluconic acid had almost no OER activities with the overpotential up to 1000 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The Fe-CDs presented improved but still poor performance with a large overpotential of 596 mV, while the overpotential of Co-CDs could be as low as 410 mV, which suggested that Co dopants could serve as more efficient active center than Fe dopants within CDs. Interestingly, the dual-metal-doped CoFe-CDs showed the most excellent OER activity with a quite low overpotential of 320 mV (with an optimized Co/Fe ratio of 7:3 (Fig. S16)), which was superior to the commercial RuO2 (420 mV), and also comparable or even lower than many reported Co, Fe-based/carbon composite electrocatalysts with complex and harsh synthetic conditions (Table S3). The Tafel slopes of the as-prepared CDs were fitted to be 84.9 mV/dec for CoFe-CDs, smaller than Co-CDs (98.5 mV/dec), RuO2 (161.8 mV/dec), Fe-CDs (182.2 mV/dec) and G-CDs (189.1 mV/dec) (Fig. 4b), indicating the more favorable electrocatalytic OER kinetic process in CoFe-CDs. Also, the obviously larger Cdl value of CoFe-CDs than those of Co-CDs, Fe-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. 4c, Fig. S17) represented the higher electrochemically active surface areas (ESCA) for OER reactions (Table S4). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) result shown in Fig. 4d demonstrated a lower charge transfer resistance of CoFe-CDs than Co-CDs, Fe-CDs and G-CDs during the OER process. Moreover, the CoFe-CDs also presented a higher TOF value (0.22 s-1, η = 0.5 V) than other CDs and the commercial RuO2 (Fig. S18). These results corroborated that the strategy of active metal doping could effectively improve the charge transfer ability and electrocatalytic activity of CDs. The synergistic effect between Co and Fe could be attributed to the modulation of the electronic structures of Co active sites by the co-doping of Fe, which was discussed in detail in our previous work [60]. To assess the electrocatalytic durability of CoFe-CDs, cycling stability test was conducted, and only 7 mV loss of overpotential was observed for CoFe-CDs after 1000 cycles (Fig. 4e). Furthermore, the i-t curve of CoFe-CDs exhibited only 3.6 % loss of current density after 12 h of continuous applied voltage at 320 mV (Fig. 4f), while 39.2 % decline was occurred for that of RuO2, suggesting the outstanding electrocatalytic OER activity and durability of the CoFe-CDs. Also, the TEM image and XRD pattern of CoFe-CDs showed no obvious changes after OER reactions (Fig. S19).

Based on the superior HER and OER performances of the CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs electrocatalysts in 1 M KOH solution, electrochemical overall water-splitting measurement was implemented by a two-electrode system in 1 M KOH electrolyte, with CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs catalysts deposited on the glassy carbon electrode as cathode and anode, respectively (Fig. 5 a inset). The LSV curve showed that a cell voltage of only 1.59 V was needed to afford a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5a), and i-t curve also remained steady during 12 h after constant applied voltage of 1.59 V (Fig. 5b), suggesting the superior and durable electrocatalytic water-splitting performance of the CoFeRu@C/CoFe-CDs system. It was concluded that the active metal doping and dual-metal synergistic effects together dominated the boost of the electrochemical activities of Ru@C and CDs, leading to the outstanding overall water electrolysis properties.

In summary, the CoFeRu@C and CoFe-CDs with superior HER and OER activities were ingeniously designed and simultaneously synthesized in one-pot hydrothermal reactor from up-supernatant and bottom-precipitate, respectively. The strategy of active transition metal doping was adequately employed here to effectively enhance the electrocatalytic activities of both CDs and Ru@C, and the impact of improvement could be greatly amplified if dual-metal dopants were adopted due to the synergistic effect. Specifically, the CoFeRu@C exhibited superior HER performance with an overpotential of 4 mV at 10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH, which was the lowest value among the previously reported noble- and non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts. Also, the CoFe-CDs possessed a low overpotential of 320 mV for OER, which was comparable to many reported complex metal/carbon hybrid electrocatalysts. Based on the CoFe-CDs/CoFeRu@C system, only 1.59 V was needed to reach 10 mA cm−2 for overall water splitting in 1 M KOH with superb stability. This work synchronously integrated the tactics of active metal doping and dual-metal synergistic effect within two different functional electrocatalysts by one-step reaction, which provided a new pathway to simply synthesize various hybrid electrocatalysts and effectively regulate the electrochemical activities for high-efficiency water splitting.

Mingxi Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Tanglue Feng: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Yixin Chen: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Junjun Liu: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Xiaohuan Zhao: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Bai Yang: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported financially by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0401701), National Science Foundation of China (51433003, 21774041), JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team (2017TD-06).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High production-yield solid-state carbon dots with tunable photoluminescence for white/multi-color light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Song, X. Liu, B. Wang, Z. Tang, S. Lu, High production-yield solid-state carbon dots with tunable photoluminescence for white/multi-color light-emitting diodes, Science Bulletin 64(23) (2019) 1788-1794.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ru-RuO2/CNT hybrids as high-activity pH-universal electrocatalysts for water splitting within 0.73 V in an asymmetric-electrolyte electrolyzer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, J. Chen, H. Li, P. Cai, Y. Li, Z. Wen, Ru-RuO2/CNT hybrids as high-activity pH-universal electrocatalysts for water splitting within 0.73 V in an asymmetric-electrolyte electrolyzer, Nano Energy 61 (2019) 576-583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic‐state cobalt and Iron Co‐doped carbon dots with superior electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen evolution reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yang, T. Feng, Y. Chen, X. Zhao, B. Yang, Ionic‐State Cobalt and Iron Co‐doped Carbon Dots with Superior Electrocatalytic Activity for the Oxygen Evolution Reaction, ChemElectroChem 6(7) (2019) 2088-2094.
